content.content:
vc-x1 0.74.0-0

Commit both repos, push the work repo's BOOKMARK, and
squash-push the bot repo's `main` — one resumable command.

Collapses the manual commit-push-publish ceremony into a
single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a
state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can
resume without re-doing completed stages.

Stages: preflight (tracking / bot-published / sync checks —
no build steps; run project checks yourself before pushing)
→ review (approve diff)
→ message ($EDITOR / --title+--body, approve text) →
commit-work → commit-bot (skipped if clean) → bookmark-set
(work repo → <bookmark>, bot repo → main) → push-work →
squash-push-bot. Failures in commit-work / commit-bot /
bookmark-set roll both repos back via
`jj op restore` to the snapshot recorded before commit-work.
After push-work succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

Non-interactive use: pass both --title and --body plus --yes
to skip the review gate. Saved state carries title/body
across resumes so only the first invocation needs them.

Usage: vc-x1 push [OPTIONS] [BOOKMARK]

Arguments:
  [BOOKMARK]
          Bookmark to advance in the work repo (positional form of
          `--bookmark`). The bot repo always advances `main`.
          
          Accepting a positional lets the common case read as `vc-x1 push main`
          without the `--bookmark` ceremony; `--bookmark` is kept as an alias
          for scripts and for composition with other args. The two forms
          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in the work repo (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --restart
          Clear any saved state file and start from stage 1

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --from <STAGE>
          Explicit stage to jump to (advanced / debug use)

          Possible values:
          - preflight:       Verify bookmark tracking, the bot-published
            invariant, and sync state
          - review:          Present diff for the first approval gate
          - message:         Compose / edit the commit message; present for
            second gate
          - commit-work:     Commit the work repo
          - commit-bot:      Commit the `.claude` bot repo (skipped if empty)
          - bookmark-set:    Advance each repo's bookmark to its `@-`: work →
            `<bookmark>`, bot → `main`
          - push-work:       `jj git push --bookmark <b> -R .`
          - squash-push-bot: In-process squash of `.claude`'s trailing session
            writes + push `main`

      --log <LOG>
          Log file path (captures all levels)

      --step
          Pause between every stage for an interactive approval gate

      --status
          Print where the saved state thinks we are and exit

      --recheck
          Re-run `preflight` even on resume (default: skip if last run
          succeeded)

      --no-squash-push
          Stop before `squash-push-bot` so it can be run manually

      --dry-run
          Print the exact commands for every stage without side effects

      --title <STR>
          Commit title (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

      --body <STR>
          Commit body (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

  -y, --yes
          Auto-approve interactive prompts (review gate, message-edit
          confirmation). Required when `--title` / `--body` aren't both supplied
          in non-interactive contexts

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

lastPrompt: let's talk about trapezoidal commits. First I'd like 0.73.0 to be a trapizodal commit, we're on a "private" branch so it's ok in that sense, but technically what are the ramifications.

content.text:
Topology confirmed — the cycle leg is intact and, importantly, **everything above `main` is mutable** (only `main` is in jj's immutable set, and it sits at `f7decbcd`, the commit just below the 0.73.0 prep). Here's the full picture.

**The operation** would be exactly the protocol's merge-non-ff recipe, applied late:

```
jj rebase -r yvtvvmvv -o main -o nqlxvkkl    # close-out ← merge(main, test fixture helpers)
```

`yvtvvmvv` is the `refactor: DRY jj facade` close-out; its parent changes from the last Work commit to the pair (`main`, last Work commit). The five-commit leg (prep + four work commits) is untouched; the three commits above (0.73.1, 0.73.2, 0.74.0-0) auto-rebase onto the new merge.

**Technical ramifications:**

- **Content is provably unchanged.** Because `main` is an ancestor of the work-tip, the merge base of the two parents *is* the work-tip — jj re-applies the close-out's diff onto it and every tree comes out byte-identical. No conflict is possible; only parent pointers and therefore SHAs change. (Git would call this a "redundant" merge — which is precisely what `--no-ff` shape means.) We can verify with `jj diff --from old --to new` being empty before pushing.
- **Change IDs survive, so every ochid keeps resolving.** The chid is what the cross-repo trailers carry, and rebase preserves it. The bot repo isn't touched at all — its linear `main`, its pairing with these pushes, and both directions of ochid links stay valid. This rewrite is the case the ochid design was built for.
- **Four git SHAs change**: the close-out plus 0.73.1, 0.73.2, and the 0.74.0-0 we just pushed. That invalidates two recorded SHAs — chores-14 refs `[6]` (close-out) and `[7]` (0.73.1). This is exactly the protocol's topic-branch warning about backfilling before permanence, now biting. Refs `[1]`–`[5]` (the untouched leg) stay valid; 0.73.2 and 0.74.0-0 have empty `Commits:` lines, so nothing else goes stale. The fix is a small re-backfill edit, which can ride in 0.74.0-1. The old GitHub URLs will likely still render for a while (unreachable objects linger) but point at abandoned commits.
- **The push is a remote rewrite.** `refactor-vc-x1@origin` moves sideways — jj pushes it force-with-lease style. Private single-user branch, no other clones, so the only cost is the stale-SHA cleanup above. `main@origin` is untouched.
- **The first-parent reading changes as intended**: `git log --first-parent` from the tip reads 0.74.0-0 → 0.73.2 → 0.73.1 → **0.73.0** → main — one entry for the cycle, work commits reachable via the second parent.
- **Landing on `main` later is unaffected**: `main` hasn't moved, so the branch can eventually land as a fast-forward, preserving these SHAs and the embedded trapezoid as-is.
- **Recovery**: the pre-rebase state stays in `jj op log`, so one `jj op restore` undoes everything local (before pushing, that is — after the force-push, recovery means another force-push).

The plain version: this is a pointer-level rewiring — no file content anywhere changes, and all the cross-repo links are built on identifiers that survive it. The only real cost is that four commits get new SHAs, two of which we'd already written down and will have to write down again after the push.

Want me to run the rebase (then show you the resulting log + empty-diff check before the force-push approval)?

message.content: So after completion of this refactor and it's all back on main will there be ochids or reference links in the history that are stale and broken?

content.text:
Short answer: the ochids all survive; the only things that can break are recorded git SHAs, and with two conditions honored, the final tip will have none stale.

**ochids — no breakage, by design.** Every ochid is a change ID, and chids ride through rebases. The trapezoid rewrite abandons the old 0.73.0 close-out commit, but its chid (`yvtvvmvv…`) moves to the rewritten merge commit — so the bot-repo commit that says `ochid: /yvtvvmvvrmkz` resolves to the new commit automatically, in any fresh clone (the chid travels inside the pushed commit object). Same for every other pair. Nothing to fix, ever, in either direction.

**Recorded SHAs — two conditions.** The `Commits:` refs in chores files carry git SHAs, which do *not* survive rewrites:

- Condition 1: after the trapezoid rewrite we re-backfill chores-14 `[6]` and `[7]` (the close-out and 0.73.1). Refs `[1]`–`[5]` point at the work-commit leg, which the merge keeps reachable via its second parent — they stay valid as-is.
- Condition 2: the program's eventual landing on `main` must be a **fast-forward or a merge**, not a squash or rebase. `main` hasn't moved and all work happens on this branch, so fast-forward (or a program-level trapezoid onto main) is the natural landing; either preserves every branch SHA, and all the backfilled refs stay correct. A squash landing would invalidate every SHA recorded on the branch — that's the one shape to rule out, and the backfills already done on this branch (technically ahead of the protocol's "wait until permanent" rule) are a bet on ruling it out.

**Unavoidable, and acceptable:** *historical versions* of the notes files — and the bot-repo session transcripts — will forever contain the superseded SHAs (e.g. `6c93a011` in the chores-14 that older commits captured). That's inherent to recording SHAs in files at all; the convention only promises the tip is correct. The old GitHub URLs for abandoned commits may also eventually 404 once GitHub garbage-collects them, and `git show <old-40-hex>` won't resolve in a fresh clone.

One genuine at-risk ref worth remembering: chores-13's ref for `0.72.0-1` points at the commit parked on `support-trapezoid-commits`. Todo #4 says "replay or redo" — if that branch is redone and deleted rather than landed, that recorded SHA becomes a permanent pointer to a commit that exists nowhere. Worth deciding deliberately when we get there.

The plain version: the cross-repo links are built on names that follow a commit through any rewrite, so they can't break. The commit *serial numbers* we wrote into two notes files do break when we rewrite, so we'll write those two down again afterward — and as long as the branch is eventually attached to main without being rewritten one more time, nothing at the end will be stale.

Want me to proceed with the rebase now?

message.content: Is it fesible to work on the branch and then before merging to main, "rebase" locally fixing all "pointers". It feels like that would work and could be automated, thoughts?

content.text:
Feasible — and the project's own conventions make it *more* automatable than the general case. Let me break it into the two versions of the idea, because one is much cheaper than the other.

**The general version (rewrite history, fixing pointers inside old commits) is solvable because all refs point backward.** The backfill rule — a SHA lands one push *after* its commit — means no commit's tree ever cites its own or a later commit's SHA. That's exactly the property that makes a single-pass rewrite converge: process commits bottom-up, and when you rewrite commit N, every SHA it could cite already has its final value in the old→new map, so you substitute in the blob and move on (this is how `git filter-repo`'s SHA remapping works). Self-reference would be a fixed-point impossibility; backward-only reference is just bookkeeping. So yes: reshape the branch however we like, then one topological pass rewrites the notes-file blobs throughout.

**But we don't actually need that version.** The convention already accepts that *historical* file versions go stale — only the tip must be correct. And after any reshape, the stale refs all sit in the tip's notes files. So "fix all pointers" collapses to: regenerate the SHA refs in the working copy and land that as an ordinary commit (or fold it into the landing/close-out commit). No history rewrite, no filter-repo machinery — the thing we're about to do by hand for `[6]`/`[7]` is the whole job.

**The automation key: make the refs re-derivable.** The obstacle to automating even the simple version is knowing the old→new SHA mapping — after a rebase that's op-log archaeology, fragile across sessions. But we already hold a durable key that survives every rewrite: the chid. If the `# References` entry records the chid alongside the SHA —

```
[6]: https://github.com/.../commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>" (chid yvtvvmvvrmkz)
```

— then the SHA becomes a *derived cache* of the chid, and a `vc-x1 fix-refs` command can re-derive every one statelessly at any time: `chid → jj log → current SHA`, rewrite the URL and title slot, done. Its dry-run twin is the `validate-refs` checker that catches stale refs in CI/pre-commit. The facade already has `cid_of`; this is a natural small command, and it slots cleanly into the refactor program — it's really a design input for the trapezoid close-out stage (`push --merge` would run fix-refs as part of landing) and pairs with the existing "validate-numbering" todo.

With that in place your proposed workflow is fully sound: backfill eagerly on the branch (nice to browse), reshape freely at landing time, run `fix-refs`, commit, land by fast-forward or merge. The one residue that stays stale by design: old file versions in history and bot-session transcripts — those are records of what was believed at the time, not live links.

The plain version: because we only ever write down the serial numbers of *earlier* commits, there's never a chicken-and-egg problem — after any reshuffle, one cleanup pass at the end can correct every number. And if each written-down number also carries the commit's permanent name (the chid), that cleanup pass becomes a dumb mechanical command instead of detective work.

Suggestion: fold both ideas — chid-in-ref format and `fix-refs`/`validate-refs` — into the trapezoid note in `tmp/` when we turn it into TODO entries. Want me to draft that now, or first run the 0.73.0 trapezoid rebase we have queued?

The goal of this tool is to preserve history and be able to use it,
so I see value in "fix-refs". And in the general case with multiple
participants rebasing is likely a requirement. Especially for non-trivial
changes and people touching the same file.

So we can't be afraid of rebasing but we need to keep it mind and
minimize issues. Thoughts?

content.text:
I think you've named the right posture — and it's worth sharpening one distinction first, because "rebasing" covers two operations with opposite relationships to your goal:

- **Reshaping** (what we're doing to 0.73.0): parents get rewired, trapezoids form, branches rebase onto a moved trunk — but every real commit stays reachable. History is preserved; only its *addresses* change.
- **Squashing**: the work commits are genuinely discarded. History is destroyed, not re-addressed.

A tool whose goal is preserving and *using* history should embrace the first freely and treat the second as a deliberate exception. Once you've made that split, "don't be afraid of rebasing" becomes a concrete engineering requirement: **links must be rewrite-tolerant by construction**, because in the multi-participant world reshaping is constant — topic branches chase trunk, and the people touching the same files are exactly the ones rebasing most.

The defense has three layers, two of which this project already has half-built:

1. **Durable keys as source of truth, addresses as derived cache.** The chid survives every jj rewrite; the SHA is the git-native, URL-resolvable address that doesn't. Record both, treat the SHA as regenerable. That's the chid-in-ref format from earlier.
2. **The backward-only invariant.** Recorded pointers only ever name the past (the backfill rule already enforces this). It's quietly load-bearing: it's what guarantees any repair pass converges in one sweep, with no circularity. Worth writing down as a stated invariant rather than an accident of the backfill timing.
3. **Repair and validation as cheap commands.** `fix-refs` heals; `validate-refs` detects, and belongs in push preflight so staleness is caught at the moment it's cheapest — before publishing — rather than by a future reader. In a multi-user setting, everyone's preflight checking link integrity is what keeps the shared record trustworthy with no coordination.

One honest caveat for the general multi-participant case: chid durability depends on the rewrite being **jj-aware**. jj carries the change ID through its own rebases, but a git-only collaborator's `git rebase` — or a forge's squash/rebase merge button — re-creates commits without it. So the multi-user contract needs two clauses: prefer landing shapes the forge doesn't rewrite (fast-forward, true merge — which the trapezoid conveniently is), and accept that when a chid does get severed, you fall back to heuristic re-linking. The current ref format is already decent for that fallback — the recorded title and 40-hex give `git log --grep` and content-matching something to work with. `validate-refs` is what tells you a severance happened at all.

The residue that no scheme fixes — old file versions and session transcripts holding superseded addresses — is fine precisely *because* of layer 3: those are honest records of what was true when written, and the tip is continuously repaired.

The plain version: there are two kinds of history rewriting — moving the furniture versus burning it. We should move furniture without fear, rarely burn, and make every written-down pointer either survive the move on its own (the permanent name) or be cheap to re-look-up afterward (the address, fixed by a command instead of by hand).

I'd fold this whole frame — reshape-vs-squash, the three layers, the mixed-tooling caveat — into the trapezoid note when we turn it into TODO entries. Shall I draft that incorporation, or run the 0.73.0 rebase first?

3. Fix the pointers **in the working copy**: re-backfill `[6]`/`[7]` with the post-rebase SHAs (which are final as soon as the rebase runs — nothing we commit above them changes them). This is a hand-run of exactly what `fix-refs` would automate.
4. That edit rides in the same docs commit as the discussion capture, at the tip.
5. Force-push the branch (with your approval at that moment).

So afterward: **the tip's files have all-valid pointers, and every commit's ochid trailers are valid — but old commits' *file versions* still contain the superseded SHAs.** That last part is not fixable at the tip and we deliberately don't chase it (it would mean rewriting every historical tree, filter-repo style — possible, per the earlier discussion, but the convention says the tip is the record that must be true). content.text:
You've caught a real inconsistency in my position — and thinking it through, per-commit fixing on this branch is not just desirable, it's nearly *free*, because of a closure property I under-weighted:

**Any commit containing a stale ref is already in the rewrite set.** A ref only goes stale when its target is rewritten. Refs are backward-only, so the citing commit is always a descendant of the cited one — and a rebase rewrites all descendants of whatever it touches. So the commits whose trees need blob fixes are *exactly* commits getting new SHAs anyway. Fixing their file contents while they're being rewritten adds **zero** additional identity churn, ever. The tip-only shortcut isn't cheaper in SHA terms — it just saves a little editing effort, at the price of historical snapshots that lie.

Concretely for this rebase, bottom-up:

1. Trapezoid rebase — the close-out's new SHA is now final.
2. Edit `0.73.1` in place: fix `[6]` (it backfilled the close-out's SHA). Its SHA is now final.
3. Edit `0.73.2` in place: fix `[7]` (it backfilled 0.73.1's SHA). `[6]` it inherits already-fixed, since its own diff never touched that line.
4. `0.74.0-0` auto-rebases and inherits both fixed lines — its diff touched neither.
5. The tip is correct *with no separate tip fix* — the fix propagated up the same way the staleness would have.

Refs `[1]`–`[5]` cite the preserved work-commit leg, which isn't rewritten, so they stay valid in every tree untouched. Message bodies (and thus ochid trailers) ride through tree edits unchanged. Result: **every commit on the branch has resolving refs**, which for a tool whose point is *using* history — bisecting, browsing an old commit on the forge — is the guarantee actually worth having.

Two honest qualifiers. First, the guarantee is per-reshape: the *next* rewrite re-stales things, and per-commit validity must be re-established each time — sustainable only because it automates (this is what `fix-refs` should be: a bottom-up pass over the rewritten set, with tip-only as the trivial case, and the closure property is the theorem that makes it always safe). Second, it's mildly ahistorical — 0.73.1's tree will claim it always knew the trapezoid SHA. That's the same ahistoricity rebasing already embraces for content; I'd accept it and note it in the trapezoid doc.

The plain version: I was wrong that fixing only the latest version is the cheap option. The commits whose written-down numbers go stale are always the same commits getting renumbered anyway — so correcting their files costs nothing extra, and afterward every version of the record is right, not just the newest one.

Updated procedure: rebase → fix `[6]` in 0.73.1 → fix `[7]` in 0.73.2 → verify (log shape, empty content-diff apart from the two ref lines, all refs resolve) → then the capture commit (trapezoid note + this design + Todo entry) at the tip — and pause for your approval before the force-push. Proceed?
